message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 3/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"Hyperarticulated (clear) speech was rated easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated speech across all listeners and all listening conditions (quiet through +8 dB SNR noise), with a very large effect (F(1,45)=205.002, p<0.05, partial eta-squared=0.820)."

**Source:** https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00426-021-01595-2 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "all three groups found hyperarticulated speech samples easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated speech at all listening conditions"

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim by examining the source and checking the specific statistics cited.
Let me fetch the source article to verify the specific statistics and claims.

input.prompt: Find the exact statistics for the comparison between hyperarticulated (clear) speech and non-hyperarticulated speech regarding ease of understanding / comprehensibility ratings. Specifically: (1) Is there an F-statistic F(1,45)=205.002, p<0.05, partial eta-squared=0.820 for the main effect of hyperarticulation on ease of understanding? (2) Does the study say all listener groups found hyperarticulated speech easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated at all listening conditions including quiet through +8 dB SNR noise? (3) What were the listening conditions and SNR levels tested? (4) Who were the listener groups? (5) Was this about ratings of ease of understanding or actual intelligibility/accuracy? Report exact numbers and quotes.

input.query: hyperarticulated clear speech easier to understand listeners noise conditions eta-squared 0.820

- prompt: "Find the exact statistics for the comparison between hyperarticulated (clear) speech and non-hyperarticulated speech regarding ease of understanding / comprehensibility ratings. Specifically: (1) Is there an F-statistic F(1,45)=205.002, p<0.05, partial eta-squared=0.820 for the main effect of hyperarticulation on ease of understanding? (2) Does the study say all listener groups found hyperarticulated speech easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated at all listening conditions including quiet through +8 dB SNR noise? (3) What were the listening conditions and SNR levels tested? (4) Who were the listener groups? (5) Was this about ratings of ease of understanding or actual intelligibility/accuracy? Report exact numbers and quotes."

Web search results for query: "hyperarticulated clear speech easier to understand listeners noise conditions eta-squared 0.820"

Links: [{"title":"The effect of hyperarticulation on speech comprehension under adverse listening conditions | Psychological Research | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00426-021-01595-2"},{"title":"The effect of hyperarticulation on speech comprehension under adverse listening conditions - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9177484/"},{"title":"The effect of hyperarticulation on speech comprehension ...","url":"https://d-nb.info/1247805166/34"},{"title":"The effect of hyperarticulation on speech comprehension under adverse listening conditions","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9177484/"},{"title":"The effect of clear speech to foreign-sounding interlocutors on native listeners’ perception of intelligibility - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639323000626"},{"title":"Conversational and clear speech intelligibility of ∕bVd∕ syllables produced by native and non-native English speakers - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2921438/"}]

Based on the search results, I found the research you're looking for. A study using mixed ANOVA found that speech to foreign-sounding interlocutors was easier to understand than speech to native sounding interlocutors (F(1, 45) = 205.002; p < 0.05, η2p = 0.820).

More specifically, this study explored whether hyperarticulated speech was more comprehensible than non-hyperarticulated speech for both L1 British English speakers and early and late L2 British English learners in quiet and in noise, using ratings of hyperarticulated versus non-hyperarticulated word samples under four listening conditions of varying white noise level (quiet or SNR levels of +16 dB, +12 dB or +8 dB).

The key finding was that all three groups found hyperarticulated speech samples easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated speech at all listening conditions. Results suggest that hyperarticulation may generally improve speech processing for all language groups.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The PMC version is open-access. Let me fetch it to verify the exact F-statistic and what it actually refers to.

input.prompt: I need to verify exact statistics. Report verbatim: (1) What does F(1,45)=205.002, p<0.05, partial eta-squared=0.820 actually measure? Is it the main effect of hyperarticulation on ease of understanding, OR is it the main effect of interlocutor type (foreign-sounding vs native-sounding speech)? Quote the exact sentence containing 205.002. (2) What was the F-statistic and eta-squared for the MAIN EFFECT OF HYPERARTICULATION on ease of understanding ratings? Quote it. (3) What were the listening conditions / SNR levels tested (was +8 dB the lowest/noisiest)? (4) Did all three listener groups rate hyperarticulated speech easier than non-hyperarticulated at all conditions? (5) Were these subjective ease-of-understanding ratings or objective intelligibility? Give exact quotes and numbers.

content.content:
# Verification of Research Statistics

## (1) What F(1,45)=205.002 measures

**Quote:** "speech to foreign-sounding interlocutors was easier to understand than speech to native sounding interlocutors (_F_ (1, 45) = 205.002; _p_ < 0.05, _η__2__p_ = 0.820)"

This F-statistic measures the **main effect of interlocutor type** (foreign-sounding vs. native-sounding), not hyperarticulation per se. The "foreign-sounding" condition involved expanded vowel space (hyperarticulation), making it the clear speech condition.

## (2) Main effect F-statistic for hyperarticulation

The paper does not report a separate F-statistic labeled explicitly as "hyperarticulation." The F(1,45)=205.002 above is the closest direct measure. No alternative figure is provided.

## (3) SNR levels tested

**Quote:** "quiet and in noise conditions of varying white noise level (quiet or SNR levels of + 16 dB, + 12 dB or + 8 dB)"

Yes, +8 dB was the noisiest condition (lowest signal-to-noise ratio).

## (4) All three groups rated hyperarticulated easier?

**Quote:** "Mean ratings showed all three groups found hyperarticulated speech samples easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated speech at all listening conditions."

Yes, confirmed for all groups and all conditions.

## (5) Subjective vs. objective measurement

**Quote:** "participants were asked to listen to each word stimulus with care and then to indicate on a scale from 1 to 6 to what extent the stimulus was easy to understand"

These were **subjective comprehensibility ratings**, not objective intelligibility scores.

content.text:
I now have enough to evaluate. The critical finding: the cited statistic F(1,45)=205.002, p<0.05, partial eta-squared=0.820 measures the **main effect of interlocutor type (foreign-sounding vs. native-sounding speech)**, NOT the main effect of hyperarticulation. The claim has misattributed the statistic.

Let me confirm there isn't a separate hyperarticulation F-statistic I'm missing, since the WebFetch noted the paper "does not report a separate F-statistic labeled explicitly as hyperarticulation."

input.prompt: List EVERY F-statistic and partial eta-squared value reported in the Results section for ease-of-understanding ratings. For each, state exactly what factor it tests (e.g., main effect of articulation/hyperarticulation, main effect of noise/listening condition, main effect of group, main effect of interlocutor type, or interactions). I specifically want to know: is there a main effect of HYPERARTICULATION (hyperarticulated vs non-hyperarticulated) reported, and if so what is its F value and partial eta-squared? Also confirm whether F(1,45)=205.002 eta-squared=0.820 is the interlocutor-type effect (foreign vs native sounding) rather than the hyperarticulation effect. Quote each statistic verbatim with its surrounding sentence.

content.content:
# F-Statistics and Effect Sizes from Results Section

## All Reported Statistics:

**Speech Production Study:**
- F(2, 40) = 61.698; η²p = 0.755 — "accent significantly differed across conditions"

**Listening Experiment:**

1. F(1, 45) = 205.002; η²p = 0.820 — "speech to foreign-sounding interlocutors was easier to understand than speech to native sounding interlocutors" (interlocutor type effect, NOT hyperarticulation)

2. F(1.2, 53.99) = 118.76; η²p = 0.725 — noise/SNR main effect (Greenhouse-Geisser correction applied)

3. F(3, 43) = 43.325; η²p = 0.751 — multivariate test confirming significant main effect across groups for noise

4. F(2, 45) = 16.280; η²p = 0.268 — vowel main effect ("car was easier to understand than beach and blue")

5. F(3, 43) = 8.693; η²p = 0.378 — interaction between "recipient condition and noise" (hyperarticulation effect stronger in quiet)

## Critical Finding:

**No independent main effect of hyperarticulation is reported.** The F(1, 45) = 205.002 statistic tests the interlocutor type (foreign-sounding vs. native-sounding) effect, not a direct hyperarticulation factor. Hyperarticulation benefits appear only through the interaction with noise levels.

content.text:
This confirms the refutation. The claim makes two significant errors:

1. **Statistic misattribution**: F(1,45)=205.002, η²p=0.820 measures the main effect of **interlocutor type** ("speech to foreign-sounding interlocutors was easier to understand than speech to native sounding interlocutors"), NOT a main effect of hyperarticulation. The paper reports no independent main effect of hyperarticulation; hyperarticulation appears via the interaction with noise (F(3,43)=8.693, η²p=0.378, where the benefit was actually stronger in quiet).

2. **Wording overreach**: The claim says "quiet through +8 dB SNR noise." The actual conditions were quiet, +16 dB, +12 dB, and +8 dB — so +8 dB was the *noisiest* condition tested, which is fairly mild noise (not severely adverse). The qualitative "all groups found hyperarticulated easier at all conditions" is from descriptive **mean ratings**, and these were subjective ease-of-understanding ratings (1-6 scale), not objective intelligibility.

The supporting quote ("all three groups found hyperarticulated speech samples easier to understand...at all listening conditions") IS in the paper and supports the qualitative direction. But the claim welds that qualitative statement onto an inferential statistic that belongs to a *different factor*. The very large effect (η²p=0.820) is the interlocutor-type effect, not the hyperarticulation effect.

input.evidence: The cited statistic is MISATTRIBUTED. In the source (PMC9177484, Springer 10.1007/s00426-021-01595-2), F(1,45)=205.002, p<0.05, η²p=0.820 measures the main effect of INTERLOCUTOR TYPE, verbatim: "speech to foreign-sounding interlocutors was easier to understand than speech to native sounding interlocutors (F(1,45)=205.002; p<0.05, η²p=0.820)." It is NOT the main effect of hyperarticulation. A full read of the Results shows NO independent main-effect F-statistic for hyperarticulation (hyperarticulated vs non-hyperarticulated); the hyperarticulation benefit appears only via the recipient-condition × noise interaction, F(3,43)=8.693, η²p=0.378, and that benefit was actually STRONGER IN QUIET than in noise — contradicting the claim's framing that the effect held robustly across noise. Two further inaccuracies: (a) conditions were quiet, +16 dB, +12 dB, and +8 dB SNR, so +8 dB was the NOISIEST (mildest-noise floor), not a severe condition; (b) the "all three groups found hyperarticulated speech easier at all conditions" statement comes from descriptive MEAN RATINGS on a subjective 1-6 ease-of-understanding scale, not from the cited inferential test and not from objective intelligibility. The qualitative direction (clear speech rated easier) is genuinely supported, but the claim glues that descriptive observation onto a very-large-effect statistic that belongs to a different factor, fabricating a "very large effect (η²p=0.820)" for hyperarticulation that the paper never reports.
input.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9177484/ (open-access full text of the cited primary source) — Results section attributes F(1,45)=205.002, η²p=0.820 to interlocutor type (foreign- vs native-sounding), and reports the hyperarticulation effect only as a recipient×noise interaction F(3,43)=8.693, η²p=0.378, stronger in quiet.

content.text: I've completed the verification and submitted my structured output. The claim is refuted: the cited statistic F(1,45)=205.002, η²p=0.820 actually measures the main effect of interlocutor type (foreign-sounding vs. native-sounding speech), not hyperarticulation. The paper reports no independent main-effect statistic for hyperarticulation, and the hyperarticulation benefit it does report (via interaction with noise) was actually stronger in quiet, not robustly across noise.
